title: Intermediate Project
summary: Build a command-line task manager application that stores tasks in a JSON file, using classes, interfaces, generics, and error handling

sections.title: Project Overview — Task Manager CLI
sections.content: You are building a command-line task manager that can add, list, complete, and delete tasks, persisting them to a JSON file. This project combines everything from intermediate lessons: interfaces define task shapes, generic storage handles loading/saving, classes encapsulate business logic, file I/O persists data, and error handling makes the app robust. The architecture follows separation of concerns: a TaskEngine class manages operations, a FileStorage generic class handles persistence, and a CLI layer handles user interaction. This is the same architectural pattern used by real todo apps, issue trackers, and project management tools — scaled down but structurally identical.

sections.title: Task Interface and Generic Storage Engine
sections.content: Shows the core Task interface and the generic storage class that handles file persistence

sections.title: Architecture — How the Pieces Fit Together
sections.content: The TaskManager class wraps a FileStore<Task> and adds domain-specific methods like 'markComplete(id)' and 'listByStatus(completed: boolean)'. The CLI layer reads commands from process.argv or a prompt library. FileStore is generic so it could store any entity (users, projects, notes) with minimal changes. Error handling wraps every file operation. This layered design — data access (FileStore), business logic (TaskManager), and presentation (CLI) — is called 'three-layer architecture'. It is the same pattern used in enterprise applications, just on a smaller scale. Each layer only knows about the layer directly below it, which makes testing and swapping implementations straightforward.

sections.title: Mixing Business Logic with File I/O Operations
sections.content: The #1 intermediate project mistake is coupling business logic directly with file operations — writing task management code and file reading code in the same function. Wrong approach: 'function addTask(title: string) { const data = fs.readFileSync("tasks.json"); ... fs.writeFileSync(...); }' in one function. Correct: separate into FileStore (handles file I/O) and TaskManager (handles business logic). This way you can unit test TaskManager without touching the filesystem, and you can swap FileStore for a DatabaseStore later without changing any business logic. If a function both calculates and persists, split it.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Complete the task manager by implementing the TaskManager class with these methods: 'addTask(title: string): Task' generates a unique ID and creates a task, 'listTasks(showCompleted?: boolean): Task[]' filters by completion status, 'markComplete(id: string): void' sets completed to true, and 'deleteTask(id: string): void' removes the task. Also add a method 'getStats(): { total: number; completed: number; pending: number }'. Each method should call the underlying FileStore methods. Handle edge cases: duplicate IDs, removing non-existent tasks, and empty task lists.
hints: How do you generate a unique string ID without external libraries — what could you use?
What should markComplete do if the task ID does not exist — throw an error or silently ignore?
How does the generic constraint 'T extends { id: string }' protect the FileStore methods?
